fulltext:
MCNAB J:

This matter came before me on an interim basis to determine the threshold issue pursuant to s 65DAAA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) in relation to the parenting arrangements for the children, X born in 2016 (aged 10) and Y born in 2018 (aged 8) (“the children”).
The applicant father is Mr Dershowitz born in 1990 (aged 35) and the respondent mother is Ms Agate born in 1996 (aged 29).

BACKGROUND

These proceedings were commenced on 28 July 2025 when the father filed an Initiating Application seeking the reconsideration of final parenting orders made on 21 February 2024 by Judge Carty.
On 27 August 2025 the matter was set down for an interim hearing on 3 December 2025 before a senior judicial registrar in relation to the discreet issue pursuant to s 65DAAA of the Act. Further orders were made releasing the Family Report of Dr B dated 5 May 2023 to the father’s treating psychologist and/or psychiatrist and for the parties to undergo hair follicle testing.
On 16 October 2025 orders were made for the Child Impact Report of Court Child Expert, Ms C (“the CCE”), dated 24 August 2022 to be provided to the father’s treating psychologist.
On 30 October 2025 the father filed an Application in a Proceeding seeking the s 65DAAA hearing be vacated. A Notice of Discontinuance in relation to this application was filed 9 November 2025.
In any case, on 3 December 2025 the s 65DAAA hearing was vacated and marked not reached due to the parties indicating that they wanted to cross-examine one another and the expert for the father, in circumstances where it was considered appropriate for this to occur. The hearing was listed before a judge of this Court on 26 March 2026, along with various directions. On this date, a Child Impact Report (“the Child Impact Report”) was also ordered, which was prepared by the CCE and released on 19 March 2026.
On 6 March 2026 the matter was transferred to the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 1) pursuant to s 149 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia Act 2021 (Cth) and listed before myself on 26 March 2026.

Hearing before Judge Carty

The initial proceedings were commenced by the mother on 12 May 2022, and the matter was heard for one day before Judge Carty on 6 February 2024. The father filed a Notice of Discontinuance on 2 February 2024, and a letter from the father’s then solicitor of the same date was tendered, informing the Court the father was no longer seeking any orders in the proceedings. The hearing proceeded on an undefended basis. Her Honour delivered judgment on 21 February 2024, making the following final parenting orders in line with the mother and ICL’s proposals:
The mother have sole parental responsibility for the children, [X], born [in 2016] and [Y], born [in 2018] (collectively “the children”).
2. The children live with the mother.

3. The children will not spend time with, or communicate with, the father.
Pursuant to s68B of the Family Law Act 1975 and for the protection of the mother and the children, the father is hereby restrained from:
Attending or removing the children from their school, sporting activities or extra-curricular activities;
Attending or removing the children from any person providing care to the children;
C. Attending upon the mother’s residence; and

D. Attending upon the mother’s place of employment.
Order 4 hereto is an injunction for the personal protection of the mother and the children to which s.68C of the Family Law Act 1975 applies, and accordingly any police officer made aware of these Orders, and who on reasonable grounds believes that such injunctions have been breached by the father, including by causing or threatening to cause bodily harm to the mother or the children, or by harassing, molesting or stalking the mother or the children, may arrest the father without warrant and will follow the steps in s.114AA of the Family Law Act 1975.
For the purpose of s65Y of the Family Law Act 1975, the mother is permitted to travel internationally with the children.
The appointment of the Independent Children’s Lawyer in these proceedings is discharged upon the expiration of 28 days after the date of these orders.
THE COURT NOTES THAT:
In the exercise of her sole parental responsibility for the children pursuant to Order 1 hereto, the mother is permitted to obtain and renew the passports for each of the children without the written consent of the father.
In the exercise of her sole parental responsibility for the children pursuant to Order 1 hereto, the mother is permitted to relocate the ordinary residence of the children to [Town D] and enroll them in a school in that location.
The making of these Final Orders has the explicit effect of discharging Order 1 of the Interim Orders made by this Court on 14 August 2023.
In the event that the father seeks to communicate with or spend time with the children then he is required to make an application to the Court.
The Independent Children’s Lawyer makes no application for costs in this matter.
The reasons for these orders are explained at [61]–[62], [70] and [73] of the judgment where her Honour said:
The father’s decision not to participate in the proceedings carries with it another layer of concern. The father's failure to participate in the hearing has left the court in a position where there are serious concerns about the level of risk that the father poses to the children, given his mental health issues, his threats to harm himself and [Mr E] and perhaps some other person, and the potential for the father to harm the children by continuing to expose them to his view, or his purported belief, that they have been inappropriately dealt with, and there is no current evidence or, at least, no cogent evidence about the father's current state of his mental health, and he has made, what the court considers, a rash decision to withdraw from the proceedings. There has been no opportunity to hear the father's evidence, or to test his evidence, to reach a concluded view as to precisely what level of risk that he poses.
On the current state of the evidence the court has come to the view that there are considerable risks for the children in the father's care. The father's decision not to participate in the hearing and not to seek any orders has not in any way ameliorated the risks to the children, and the court can have no confidence that the father has adequately addressed his mental health issues.
...

PARENTAL RESPONSIBILITY

...
Due to the threats which the father has made to harm the mother's partner, and the lack of evidence as to the current state of his mental health and whether he has adequately addressed his significant mental health challenges, it would not be reasonable, in my view, for the mother to have to consult with the father, and to have her endeavour to come to a joint decision with him about major long-term issues for the children.

...
In view of the father having made threats to harm the mother's partner, and in view of the father having withheld the children from the mother in May 2022, I consider that it is appropriate for the welfare of the children to make the restraints sought by the mother and the Independent Children's Lawyer pursuant to section 68B of the Act, and I also consider that it is appropriate to make an order pursuant to section 68C of the Act.
Although her Honour considered that the children would benefit from a meaningful relationship with both parents, it was found at [63] that the children were at an unacceptably high risk of harm if they were to spend time with the father, both psychologically due to alleged coaching from the father that one of the children was being sexually harmed by the mother’s partner, Mr E, and potentially physically as a result of the father’s mental health and possible alcohol issues.
The Court accepted that the father’s ongoing mental‑health difficulties, including suicidal ideation, threats of violence, and limited engagement with any assistance, presented a serious and unresolved risk to the children. His minimisation of past incidents and evidence to the contrary undermined confidence that those risks have been properly addressed. In particular, the Court gave little weight to a psychologist’s report relied upon by the father because it was based entirely on his self‑reporting and his self-report was somewhat inaccurate.
In relation to whether the children should spend supervised time with the father, her Honour determined at [64]-[65] that this would not meet the current needs of the children as the father has not demonstrated that he has, or will be able to, address his parenting capacity.
At [67] her Honour commented:
If in future the father can demonstrate that he has adequately addressed the risk issues the court has identified, and that he will not expose the children to risk of harm, then the matter may have to be revisited. It is a sad situation in this case that there is no order that the Court can make that is least likely to lead to the risk of further litigation, given that the father may be unhappy with the court's decision, but there is simply no evidence upon which I could find, at this stage, that the children would be safe in his care.

PROPOSALS

As noted above, the father seeks that the final parenting orders be reconsidered. The father seeks consequently on that as set out in his Initiating Application, orders that the children spend equal time with him and the mother. It was said by his counsel that he would be urging the father to seek different orders, however, it was not made clear what those orders would be.
In line with her Response to Initiating Application, the mother seeks that the Initiating Application be dismissed and an order for costs.

EVIDENCE

By way of his Outline of Case, the father says that in support of his case he relies upon the following:
(a) consolidated affidavit of Mr Dershowitz filed on 17 March 2026;

(b) annexures A, B, C & D of the affidavit of Mr Dershowitz filed 17 March 2026;

(c) affidavit of Dr F filed 30 October 2025;

(d) annexures DRF-1 & DRF-2 of the affidavit of Dr F filed 30 October 2025;

(e) affidavit of Ms G filed 23 July 2025;

(f) affidavit of Mr H filed 23 July 2025;

(g) final orders of Judge Carty dated 21 February 2024;

(h) reasons for judgment of Judge Carty dated 21 February 2024; and

(i) applicant’s tender bundle, pages 3-5, 281, 282-301; 17-18, 45-48, 49-51, 52-54; 76-77.

By way of her Outline of Case, the mother says that in support of her case she relies upon the following:
(a) affidavit of Ms Agate filed 20 March 2026;

(b) Child Impact Report of Ms C filed 9 March 2026;

(c) affidavit of Ms J filed 16 October 2025;

(d) Notice of Child Abuse, Family Violence or Risk filed 26 August 2025;

(e) Family Report of Dr B dated 5 May 2023; and

(f) Child Impact Report of Ms C dated 24 August 2022.

The parties and Dr F were required for cross-examination.

RELEVANT LAW

The application to reconsider parenting orders is governed by the operation of s 65DAAA of the Act which provides:
(1) If a final parenting order is in force in relation to a child, a court must not reconsider the final parenting order unless:
(a) the court has considered whether there has been a significant change of circumstances since the final parenting order was made; and

(b) the court is satisfied that, in all the circumstances (and taking into account whether there has been a significant change of circumstances since the final parenting order was made), it is in the best interests of the child for the final parenting order to be reconsidered.

(2) For the purposes of determining whether the court is satisfied as mentioned in paragraph (1)(b), and without limiting section 60CC, the court may have regard to any matters that the court considers relevant, including the following:

(a) the reasons for the final parenting order and the material on which it was based;

(b) whether there is any material available that was not available to the court that made the final parenting order;

(c) the likelihood that, if the final parenting order is reconsidered, the court will make a new parenting order that affects the operation of the final parenting order in a significant way (whether by varying, discharging or suspending the final parenting order, in whole or in part, or in some other way);

(d) any potential benefit, or detriment, to the child that might result from reconsidering the final parenting order.

(3) Despite subsection (1), the court may reconsider a final parenting order with the agreement or consent of all the parties to that order.

(4) The failure of a court to comply with subsection (1) does not affect the validity of any order made by the court.

This section codifies the principles established in Rice & Asplund (1979) FLC 90-725 and the cases applying it. As was noted in Carlyon & Graham [2024] FedCFamC1F 443 at [46]- [48] by Schonell J:
There is nothing new in the provisions contained in s 65DAAA rather it is a codification of well-established jurisprudence.
It has been clearly articulated in numerous authorities that the court should not “lightly entertain an application to reverse” an earlier order unless it is satisfied that there are changed circumstances It was sometimes inelegantly described as the rule in Rice & Asplund.
The rationale for the existence of the so-called rule is that the best interests of children are not promoted by endless litigation and that once the court has made a determination, unless there has been established a change of circumstances, then the court should not engage in further litigation in relation to the child. In that respect as long ago as in Freeman and Freeman [1986] FamCA 23; (1987) FLC 91-857, Strauss J said at 76-470–76-471:
... Once the court ... has settled the question of custody, it is usually in the interests of the children that the order made by the court is treated as determining the dispute and be given the necessary support. Stability in the lives of children and also in the lives of adults is an essential prerequisite to their well-being. ...
That approach to the interpretation of the section was approved in Radecki & Radecki [2024] FedCFamC1A 246; (2024) FLC 94-232 at [79] per Austin & Williams JJ and at [117]-[129] per Carew J (‘Radecki’).
In making any parenting order, s 60CA of the Act governs that the child’s best interests is the paramount consideration and in determining the best interests I have regard to the matters set out at s 60CC of the Act:
(2) For the purposes of paragraph (1)(a), the court must consider the following matters:
(a) what arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of:
(i) the child; and

(ii) each person who has care of the child (whether or not a person has parental responsibility for the child);

(b) any views expressed by the child;

(c) the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child;

(d) the capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs;

(e) the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so;

(f) anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child

When considering the matters at s 60CC(2)(a), s 60CC(2A) sets out that the court must consider any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child and any family violence order that applies or has applied to the child or a member of the child’s family.

CONSIDERATION

Section 65DAAA(1)(a)

The court has considered whether there has been a significant change of circumstances since the final parenting order was made

In his affidavit filed 18 March 2026, the father deposes that there has been a significant change of circumstances both for himself and the children which require reconsideration of the final parenting orders.

Children’s Exposure to Drugs and Family Violence

The father alleges that the children are being exposed to illicit drugs in the mother’s home through her partner, Mr E. The father says that there is NSW Police evidence that Mr E uses an illicit substance, including being under the influence whilst driving with the children.[1] The father also says there was a mandatory report made in July 2024 that records the youngest child attending school with a cut and bruised eye, reportedly caused by Mr E,[2] although DCJ report that this injury was likely the result of an accident.[3]
On 30 May 2025 the father’s partner, Ms G, and the father’s friend, Mr H, say that they were in a conversation with the father and another man, Mr K in which they discussed that Mr E had purchased a large quantity of an illicit substance with the intention to sell, and unregistered firearms.[4] The affidavit of Mr H is hearsay evidence of conversations with Mr K. The subpoenaed police records indicate that the police hold no concerns in regards to Mr E being a drug dealer or excessive user as identified at paragraph 18 of the Child Impact Report.
The mother in her affidavit filed 20 March 2026 denied these allegations.[5] The mother says that the father has continued to threaten and harass her family and colleagues.[6]

Father’s Mental Health

The father says that since the final parenting orders were made, his mental health issues which he acknowledges in his affidavit evidence were significant (albeit in his words “situational”) have resolved.[7] In oral evidence before the Court he confirmed that he had told the CCE in 2022 that he held no concerns regarding his mental health and repeated comments to that effect when meeting with her in February 2026 for the preparation of the latest Child Impact Report. On that occasion that father advised the CCE that he did not feel it necessary to attend upon a psychologist including engaging with Dr F from September to October 2025.[8] In oral evidence, he accepted that he went to Dr F for assessment rather than to deal with issues including childhood separations from his mother and his issues with the mother and Mr E. The father continues to hold very negative views of Mr E and the mother. In oral evidence he conceded that there has been no change in his circumstances in relation to his attitude towards the mother and Mr E.
The father relied upon the report of Dr F, who he attended upon for five sessions between 3 September 2025 and 16 October 2025.[9] He commenced seeing Dr F after he had initiated these proceedings seeking a reconsideration of the final parenting orders. The father also says he continues to attend upon Dr F from time to time, although did not provide a frequency for these visits.[10] The mother contends the father has not completed any long-term psychological therapy nor parenting courses as recommended by the previous Family Report writer, Dr B.[11] It was indicated in the Child Impact Report that the father is dismissive of the benefit of these courses and disagrees that he should attend these.[12]The father gave oral evidence that he may have attended three courses but could not remember what they were. Plainly, these courses were not impactful for the father.
The father also says that his business has been recovering,[13] he is in a committed relationship with Ms G,[14] and has relocated closer to his work and family.[15] These are said to indicate that the father has had significant change in his own circumstances which have led to an improvement of his condition and capacity.
As to the report of Dr F, Dr F opined that the father did not meet the criteria for any significant mental health problems based on clinical assessments throughout five interviews, four conducted over video conference and one in-person interview, as well as results from various psychometric assessments.
In direct response to his review of court documents, Dr F administered further psychometric testing, the results of which he asserts maintains that the father currently has no mental health concerns. Dr F acknowledged that the psychometric tests were all completed without observation and addressed this by requiring the father to then complete a further test (which he described as the most complex of the tests) at his office. This final test was said to validate the earlier tests results which arose from self and remotely completed tests.
Dr F noted that as the father presented with no observed mental health issues, treatment of the father included working on strategies to manage stress in relation to court proceedings and his concerns of the welfare of the children. No detail was provided regarding the treatment and there was little evidence that Dr F (either in his report or in his evidence before the Court) had addressed the issues of the father’s thinking and behaviour which caused him to coach one of the children to make allegations of abuse against Mr E or to continue to hold very negative views of the mother and Mr E.

Section 65DAAA(2)(a)

The reasons for the final parenting order and the material on which it was based

The reasons of Judge Carty are set out above. The evidence upon which the orders were made are set out in the judgment at [40]-[67].
Her Honour noted at [47] and [51] that there was no updated assessment provided by the father in relation to his mental health at the time of the trial or evidence that he had received treatment to address these concerns. Further, that the father demonstrated behaviours during his supervised contact visits which raised concerns about his wellbeing, such as appearing withdrawn and using inappropriate language in front of the children (at [52]-[58]).
Her Honour found at [60] that the father continued to minimise his mental health issues, raising concerns about what assistance he had obtained. Additionally, the father’s decision not to participate in proceedings left his evidence untested and it could not be concluded the precise extent of the risk he posed (at [61]).

Section 65DAAA(2)(b)

Whether there is any material available that was not available to the court that made the final parenting order

As noted above, the father does not deny that his mental health issues at the time of the final hearing were significant,[16] however, he points to the provision of the report from Dr F to support his mental health improvement and as addressing the risk of harm raised by her Honour throughout the judgment. In particular, he says that Dr F considered that the concerns raised by the father’s mental health at the time of the final parenting orders have been adequately addressed.
The father also seeks to rely on material annexed to his affidavit from NSW Police and the DCJ in relation to the change of circumstances in the children’s living situation due to the risks arising from Mr E. It was noted above that the DCJ report determined the injury to Y likely as a result of an accident, although records from NSW Police substantiate Mr E’s use of an illicit substance as well as further involvement with the law enforcement such as being named a suspect in a theft and suspension of his firearm licence.[17]
In the latest Child Impact Report, the CCE identified that it was unclear whether the father had an understanding of the children’s needs,[18] and in interview, the children expressed that they did not wish to see the father.[19] The CCE suggested that further information should be received by the Court before considering varying the parenting arrangement.[20]
The affidavit evidence of the father and Dr F did not substantially add to the material already filed by the father in the earlier proceedings. The father’s continued behaviour and views of Mr E and his failure to engage in a robust way to address concerns that the Court held in relation to his mental health undermines submissions that there is fresh evidence to inform the Court about the risks presented by the father. The situation that Judge Carty was confronted with in relation to the risks presented by the father as outlined at [51] remain:
The father has minimised the events in 2022 when reporting to a health practitioner, or even a person assessing the state of his mental health for the purposes of these court proceedings, and absent the father providing any cogent evidence that he has addressed his issues, the court can have no confidence that the father has obtained therapy that may have been necessary to assist him to recover from his condition, particularly in light of him expressing, even in January 2024, his persistent belief that the children have been inappropriately dealt with by the mother's partner.
It is plain that from the terms of his report and oral evidence, Dr F did not engage in any detailed way to assist the father with the very serious issues of risk arising from the father’s mental health, attitudes and behaviour as detailed in paragraphs [45]-[62] of the judgement. There is no evidence of Dr F challenging the father’s decision to relocate to a town near where the mother now resides in circumstances where that conduct has been raised as an example of the father seeking to coercively control the mother and where the father had previously opposed the mother moving to that town.
It is said on the father’s behalf that there have been no reports of the father exhibiting signs of poor mental health since 2022, however, that submission ignores the other constellation of concerns raised by his ongoing negative views of the mother and Mr E.

Section 65DAAA(2)(c)

The likelihood that, if the final parenting order is reconsidered, the court will make a new parenting order that affects the operation of the final parenting order in a significant way (whether by varying, discharging or suspending the final parenting order, in whole or in part, or in some other way)

By this application, the father seeks for the proceedings to be reopened, and he seeks consequently on that, orders the children to spend equal time with him and the mother. As noted above, it was said by his counsel that he would be urging the father to seek different orders, however, it was not made clear what those orders would be.
Again, it is not apparent that the father has addressed the clearly identified risk factors that arose from his previous conduct. He continues to hold very strong negative views of the mother and Mr E and continues to believe that Mr E sexually abused one of the children and vandalised his equipment. He is firmly of the view that there is no need to modify his thinking or behaviour. Given those circumstances, I find it unlikely that the Court would vary the orders in a significant way on the basis of the evidence currently relied on by the father.

Section 65DAAA(2)(d)

Any potential benefit, or detriment, to the child that might result from reconsidering the final parenting order

The benefit of a meaningful relationship with both parents was strongly considered in her Honour’s judgment at [63]-[66]. The father raises concerns in his affidavit that the children may have received an inaccurate impression of him resulting in a deterioration of their relationship, and that he is willing to commit to any process necessary for him to safely maintain a meaningful relationship with the children.[21]
The CCE noted concerns if the father was perpetrating coercive control over the children, then contact with the father may increase a risk of the children being weaponised by the father and disrupting their relationship with the mother.[22]
Counsel for the father submitted that because a Child Impact Report had been ordered and the mother and children had been interviewed as part of that process, the continuation of proceedings would lead to minimal detriment given that the case was already halfway run. It was also said that it was within the power of the Court to limit the scope of any further evidence if the case was reopened. The difficulty with that submission is that the father continues to hold the view that Mr E has abused the children and has not addressed the concerns in her Honour’s judgment.

CONCLUSION

For these reasons, I do not find, first, that there has a been a significant change in circumstances for there to be a reconsideration of the final parenting orders. In any case, neither I am satisfied that even if it were to be said that there has been a significant change in the circumstances, that it would be the in the best interests of the children, having regard to the above matters and s 60CC of the Act, for the final parenting orders to be reconsidered.
The mother’s Notice of Costs filed 24 March 2026 indicates that the mother has incurred costs of $7,695 with further legal costs expected of $8,000. The mother, by way of her Outline of Case, seeks that the father pay her costs in the amount of $21,000. Without hearing argument or submissions, the quantum of the costs sought appears to be excessive.
Again, not having heard argument, I remind the parties that an order for costs on an indemnity basis is an extraordinary order, particularly in a jurisdiction where the position dictated by statute is that each party bear their own costs (s 114UB(1)). The Court will make orders in relation to any application for costs.
